name: Βρομόλιθος
subtitle: A small pebbly cove on the east side of the Greek island of Leros, five minutes' walk from the capital Platános. Clear water straight into depth, Mediterranean-tideless, with the Venetian Castle of Pandeli watching over it from the ridge to the north.
nearest_city_blurb: The main port of Leros. Ferries to Piraeus, Kos and Patmos arrive here; the island's municipal building and market are on its harbour front.
intro: Vromolithos is a pocket beach on Leros, one of the Dodecanese islands strung along Greece's frontier with Turkey. It is short, pebbly, and sits on the open east side of the island facing the Aegean. The beach does not appear in Greece's Blue Flag list and has no lifeguard or municipal services — just a handful of tavernas on the road above it and the Castle of Pandeli visible up on the ridge to the north. It is a local beach that visitors find if they stay on Leros for more than a day.

setting.paragraph: Leros is a 53 km² Greek island in the southern Aegean, 317 km from Athens's port of Piraeus and about 32 km from the Turkish mainland. The Dodecanese are what's left of a chain of volcanic-arc mountaintops that broke the surface when the Aegean basin opened in the Miocene — which is why the sand and gravel at Vromolithos is not the pale quartz of Atlantic beaches but a darker, lithic-rich mix: basalt and tuff ground up by the sea, with quartz made up from the roots of older rocks. The island's modern history is punctuated by the Italian fascist architecture at Lakki harbour, the British and Italian defence against the Germans in the Battle of Leros (November 1943), and a long second life as the setting of psychiatric hospitals — subject of Greek journalism and Samaras's 1990 film The Suspended Step of the Stork.

nearby_features.name: Pandeli
nearby_features.blurb: Fishing harbour and tavernas below the castle; the loveliest of Leros's east-side villages.

nearby_features.name: Castle of Pandeli (Kástro)
nearby_features.blurb: Byzantine-origin, Venetian- and Hospitaller-rebuilt castle on the ridge above Platános. Visible from the beach; a 15-minute climb from Pandeli harbour.

nearby_features.name: Platános
nearby_features.blurb: The capital of Leros, sitting in the saddle between the Pandeli and Agía Marína harbours.

nearby_features.name: Agía Marína
nearby_features.blurb: The main port. Ferries to Piraeus, Kos and Patmos.

nearby_features.name: HMS Intrepid (D10) wreck site
nearby_features.blurb: British Royal Navy destroyer sunk in Leros Bay on 27 September 1943, six weeks before the Battle of Leros. Still lies on the seabed between Leros and the small islet of Rina.

nearby_features.name: Lakki
nearby_features.blurb: Leros's deep-water harbour — a planned-rationalist port town built by the Italians in the 1920s, one of the most complete examples of Italian Fascist architecture outside Italy.

coastal_path.name: Platános–Pandeli–Vromolithos footpath
coastal_path.description: A short walking route links the capital Platános down to Pandeli harbour and continues south along the coast to Vromolithos. Roughly 25 minutes end-to-end. Informal, signposted from Platános.

context_photos.caption: The Castle of Pandeli seen from below — visible from Vromolithos beach, 900 m to the north-northwest.
Pandeli harbour — the fishing village just over the next headland, 650 m north.
Agía Marína, Leros's main port. 1.4 km north of Vromolithos across the Platános saddle.
The castle lit at night, seen from Platános.

context_photos_note: Vromolithos itself has not been uploaded to Wikimedia Commons. These photographs are from the adjoining villages of Pandeli, Platános and Agía Marína — all within a fifteen-minute walk.

climate.water_temp_source_note: Water-temp curve is a typical northern-Aegean monthly profile placeholder pending the sea-surface-temperature pipeline.

predicted.regime_plain: Quartz 48 % · feldspar 6 % · rock fragments 46 %. The high lithic fraction and very low feldspar are typical of an Aegean volcanic-arc coast — the sand here is basalt and tuff ground by the sea, with quartz washed down from older basement rocks. The name Vromolithos itself translates as "stinking stone," from iron-rich spring seepage along the shore.

registry_status.note: Not a Blue Flag beach and not listed on Greece's national bathing-waters registry (epb.ypeka.gr). A local and visitor-known beach without municipal services.
